1.matchText: Babylon was already the largest city in Mesopotamia
1.blurb: By 1500 BCE, Babylon under the Kassite dynasty was the dominant power in Mesopotamia and probably the largest city in the Near East, with a population in the tens of thousands. The Kassites had seized power from the weakened Old Babylonian dynasty around 1595 BCE -- almost exactly when Tang of Shang was founding a new dynasty on the Yellow River. Two new ruling houses, at opposite ends of Asia, replacing the old order at the same historical moment.

1.matchText: Mycenaean Greece was building its hilltop palaces
1.blurb: The Ancient China TL ends right where the Shang begins: Tang's victory at Mingtiao around 1600 BCE, the moment the cultural toolkit assembled over five thousand years of Neolithic development -- jade, bronze, ancestors, walls, the dynastic cycle -- finally passed into the hands of a literate dynasty that would write things down.

1.matchText: Assyrians would build trading colonies at Kanesh
1.blurb: The Assyrian trading colony at Kanesh in central Turkey (c. 1950-1750 BCE) was a commercial outpost built to access tin and textiles -- a resource-extraction colony separated from the home city of Ashur by over 600 miles of hostile territory. The Shang's Panlongcheng colony, built 500 km south of Zhengzhou to access copper and tin, is the closest East Asian parallel: same logic, same era, opposite ends of the continent.
The Shang is where Chinese legend becomes archaeology — Erlitou's successor, the first dynasty with writing you can read.
Like the Indus, the Shang built a bronze-and-city civilization; unlike it, the Shang left readable script, so we know its kings' names.
Shang divination-kingship parallels Mesopotamian temple-states — rulers as the indispensable channel between people and gods.

2.blurb: Mesopotamia's cuneiform clay tablet archives are the other great written record of the Bronze Age world. Cuneiform began around 3400 BCE -- over two thousand years before the Shang oracle bones -- as pictographic marks pressed into wet clay to track grain and labor. By the time the Shang were carving their first inscriptions, Mesopotamia had already produced the Epic of Gilgamesh, law codes, diplomatic correspondence, and centuries of administrative records.
Oracle-bone writing is the script Longshan and Liangzhu were groping toward — China's threshold of literate history.
Oracle bones are to China what cuneiform tablets are to Sumer: bureaucratic-religious record-keeping that accidentally preserved a civilization.

3.matchText: Hittite Empire, which fought the Battle of Kadesh against Egypt around 1274 BCE
3.blurb: The Hittites were the great power of Anatolia, running an empire from their capital at Hattusa that rivaled Egypt and Babylon. The Battle of Kadesh in 1274 BCE -- fought between Ramesses II and the Hittite king Muwatalli II -- is the best-documented battle of the ancient Near East, with both sides claiming victory. The Hittites worked in bronze too, but never at the ritual scale the Shang achieved.

3.matchText: death masks and cups from Shaft Graves at Mycenae
3.blurb: While the Shang were casting massive bronze ritual vessels, the Mycenaean Greeks were burying their kings with gold death masks and cups in the Shaft Graves at Mycenae. Two contemporary Bronze Age civilizations expressing royal power through completely different materials -- gold in the Aegean, bronze in the Yellow River plain -- each producing artworks of astonishing beauty in their own tradition.
Shang ritual bronzes are the technical apex of a metallurgical tradition reaching back into the painted-pottery Neolithic.

4.blurb: The Mycenaean palaces that supposedly mustered the Greek fleet for Troy were contemporary with Wu Ding's Shang at Anyang. Both civilizations were chariot-using, bronze-casting, palace-centered kingdoms at their peak in the 13th century BCE -- and both would be gone within two centuries, the Mycenaeans to the Bronze Age collapse and the Shang to the Zhou conquest.

5.matchText: Mesopotamians left food offerings at family burial sites
5.blurb: Mesopotamian ancestor reverence was a private family affair: you left food at the family grave, poured water libations, and spoke the names of your dead to keep their spirits fed. Nobody cracked bones to consult them about state policy. The Shang took ancestor worship to a completely different level -- a full-blown state religion where the king asked the dead for permission before every major decision, on an industrial scale of cracked bone and sacrificed humans that would have horrified a Sumerian.

5.matchText: Royal Tombs of Ur contained sacrificed attendants
5.blurb: The Royal Tombs of Ur (c. 2600-2400 BCE) are the most famous case of mass human sacrifice in Mesopotamia: Queen Puabi's death pit held 74 attendants -- soldiers, musicians, ladies-in-waiting -- apparently drugged and killed to accompany her. But even Ur's death pits pale beside the Shang royal tombs at Xibeigang, where oracle bones record sacrificial events of 100 to 300 people at a single ceremony.
Shang ancestor worship and royal divination is a cousin of Mesopotamian cult — the dead and the gods as an administered relationship.

6.matchText: the newly conquered Shang people that heaven had destroyed their dynasty
6.blurb: The dynastic cycle that the Ancient China TL traced from its earliest roots -- Yu the Great founding the Xia, Jie losing it through tyranny, Tang seizing the mandate at Mingtiao -- now completes its second full revolution. Di Xin plays the Jie role; King Wu plays the Tang role; the Mandate of Heaven passes again. The Shang spent five centuries telling the story of how Tang righteously overthrew a tyrant, and then watched their own last king become the tyrant in someone else's version of the same story.

The north-south axiality of the Shang palace -- ruler in the north, facing south, principal buildings aligned on a central axis -- is the same spatial grammar as Erlitou's Palace No. 1, and it would persist through every Chinese imperial capital for three thousand years. The Forbidden City in Beijing, built in the 15th century CE, still follows the template the Shang refined at Zhengzhou and Yinxu.
The Shang world — walled towns, chariots, bronze, human sacrifice — is the mature form of what Erlitou began.

7.matchText: Bronze Age collapse that devastated civilizations across the eastern Mediterranean around 1200 BCE
7.blurb: Around 1200-1150 BCE, the interconnected palace civilizations of the eastern Mediterranean collapsed in rapid sequence: Mycenaean Greece fell, the Hittite Empire disintegrated, Egypt's New Kingdom weakened, and the trading networks that linked them all went dark. Sanxingdui's abandonment around 1100-1050 BCE falls in the same global window -- whether the Sichuan Basin felt the same disruption or collapsed for local reasons is one of the great open questions of Bronze Age archaeology.

Sanxingdui shattered the old narrative that Chinese civilization began in the Yellow River valley -- with Yangshao, Longshan, and Erlitou -- and radiated outward from there. The Ancient China TL traces that Yellow River sequence through eight chapters, from Neolithic millet farmers to the Erlitou proto-state. Sanxingdui proves that Bronze Age China had multiple independent centers of complexity, not just one.
Sanxingdui's alien bronzes prove early China was not one culture but several — the Yellow River was never the only story.

8.matchText: Confucius is traditionally considered a descendant of the Shang royal house
8.blurb: Mesopotamia is the anti-China when it comes to cultural memory: same river valley, completely different peoples cycling through every few centuries -- Sumerians, Akkadians, Babylonians, Assyrians -- each inheriting the previous civilization's cities and gods but none remembering themselves as continuations of the last. The Shang, by contrast, never stopped being remembered: their writing system survived, their royal line survived in the Song state, and their most famous descendant was Confucius himself.
